nativeRange.habitat: Has been documented inhabiting rock jetties, rocky reefs, and coral-rich areas across the tropical western Atlantic, generally between roughly 3 and 35 m depth (occasionally recorded as deep as ~90 m). Juveniles tend to associate with fire corals and shelter in crevices.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding heavily on sponges, along with tunicates, zoantharians, and algae. The species' near-obligate sponge dependency is the main reason it is generally considered difficult to keep — many specimens refuse to switch to prepared foods and slowly starve. Aquarists who attempt this species are generally advised to offer sponge-based angelfish preparations as the dietary backbone, supplemented with marine algae and Spirulina, frozen mysis and finely chopped meaty items (~30–35% of intake), and to provide mature live rock for continuous grazing. Frequent small feedings (2–3 times per day) are generally recommended.

goodWith: most community fish in a large system

summary: A striking Caribbean angelfish with a bright yellow head and front body fading into a black hindquarters. Generally considered an expert-only fish due to a near-obligate sponge diet that makes long-term feeding in captivity very difficult.
careNotes: The Rock Beauty Angelfish (Holacanthus tricolor) is a western Atlantic / Caribbean angelfish ranging from Bermuda and Georgia (USA) south through the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean to Santa Catarina, Brazil. Adults are visually distinctive: a bright yellow head and front body, deep blue-ringed eyes, and a predominantly black hindquarters and tail. Juveniles display a strikingly different color phase — almost entirely bright yellow with a single blue-ringed black spot under the dorsal fin that expands and spreads as the fish matures, so juveniles and adults are easily mistaken for different fish. Maximum reported wild size on FishBase is around 35 cm TL (common length closer to 30 cm), while aquarium retailers commonly list smaller sizes of 20–25 cm (8–10 in); a working range of approximately 20–30 cm is documented here. LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 125 US gal (~473 L) and AquariumDomain ~90 US gal (~340 L); given the species' territorial behavior, need for ample live rock and rockwork, and the fact that the species reaches adult sizes well above the small-fish range that justify those numbers, a more cautious minimum of approximately 500 L (~130+ gal) is used here. Temperature recommendations vary: LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain suggest roughly 22.2–25.6°C while FishBase reports a preferred wild range of 25.4–28°C (mean 27.4°C); an averaged working range of approximately 23–27°C is documented. Specific gravity is generally kept around 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. The defining husbandry challenge is diet: in the wild the species is largely spongivorous, and most specimens have difficulty switching to aquarium foods. Many wild-caught individuals slowly starve in captivity even when accepting some prepared food, which is why this species is generally rated 'expert' / 'difficult' across sources and is widely considered unsuitable for keepers who cannot commit to sponge-based formulations and intensive feeding. Like other large Pomacanthus / Holacanthus angelfishes, the Rock Beauty is generally not fully reef-safe — it has been reported to nip stony and soft corals, zoanthids, and clam mantles. The species is typically kept singly; conspecific aggression toward other Rock Beauties (and other large angelfishes) tends to be high. FishBase notes 'protogyny has been proposed for this species awaiting confirmation' — the protogynous trait is recorded here on that basis. Large angels of this kind tend to be HLLE-susceptible, so a varied, vitamin-rich diet and stable water quality are generally advised. Lifespan is not well documented in hobby sources for this species. The designated secondary source (Reef2Reef wiki) returned HTTP 403; FishBase and AquariumDomain were used as secondaries instead.
breedingNotes: No reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol is documented for this species. In the wild Holacanthus tricolor is a pelagic spawner and is reported (awaiting confirmation per FishBase) to be a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite. Captive breeding has not been established in the hobby.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max size (35 cm TL, common 30 cm), depth range (3–92 m, usually 3–35 m), Western Atlantic distribution from Bermuda/Georgia through the Caribbean to Brazil, preferred temperature (25.4–28°C, mean 27.4°C), and sponge/tunicate/algae diet. FishBase reports that protogyny has been proposed for this species awaiting confirmation.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Designated secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) returned HTTP 403; replaced by AquariumDomain. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament, difficult care level, reef-safe-with-caution status, sponge-dominant diet (~35% other meaty items), juvenile yellow-with-eyespot color phase, and ~22.2–25.6°C / pH 8.1–8.4 / SG 1.020–1.025 / dKH 8–12. Listed minimum 90 US gallons; tank-size figure here biased larger (500 L) given territorial behavior and adult size.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner in the wild; no reliable home-aquarium protocol exists.
